coredata.dc:title: A convenient way of activating carbon quantum dots and the efficient isolation
coredata.dc:creator: Kuai, Yong
Fang, Raohao
Zhang, Changchang
Liu, Xiang
Ma, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Photoluminescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attracts extensive interests. Chemical modifications in a proper way on CQDs are vital for their applications. However, isolating the modified CQDs efficiently from the mixture is still a challenge. Conventional dialysis isolation did not effect at all since the unremarkable changes in size of the modified CQDs. Herein active N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) ester was introduced chemically onto the CQDs in very mild conditions, which was demonstrated by IR and X-ray photoelectron spectra. Such activated CQDs could be extracted by ethyl acetate from the mixture efficiently and conveniently which was confirmed by photoluminescence spectra. Experimental results also demonstrated the low cytotoxicity and the high stability against photo-bleaching of the CQDs-NHS. The high reactivity of NHS moiety was demonstrated by the reaction with bovine serum albumin, which revealed in an experiment of sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electropheresis and the fluorescence imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
N-hydroxysuccinimide
Isolation
Fluorescence labeling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CQDs and the modification on the CQDs with NHS
Activation of the CQDs with NHS and the efficient isolation
Photo-bleaching resistance of the CQDs and cytotoxicity of the CQDs-NHS
Fluorescent labeling on BSA with the CQDs-NHS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CQDs, derived from dehydration of citric acid, reacted with NHS at the presence of dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) and the NHS-functionalized CQDs (CQDs-NHS) were isolated by extraction of ethyl acetate. The separated CQDs-NHS conjugated with BSA covalently which was demonstrated by the fluorescent image.
TEM and AFM images of the CQDs shown in (a) and (b) respectively. Insets of (a) and (b) show the crystalline planes and depth histogram of the CQDs respectively.

IR spectra of the CQDs and the CQDs-NHS exhibited in (a). The alkyl absorptions of the CQDs and the CQDs-NHS are marked with a dashed rectangle. XPS with high-resolution scans on C 1 s of CQDs and CQDs-NHS correspond to (b) and (c) respectively. N 1 s spectrum of CQDs-NHS in (d) witnesses the introduction of N element in CQDs after NHS esterification.

Photographs of the CQDs and the CQDs-NHS in mixed solvents of ethyl acetate and water shown in (a) and (b) respectively. The PL spectra of the ethyl acetate extractive in (a) is disclosed in (c). The PL spectra corresponding to the ethyl acetate and the water extractive in (b) are shown in (d).

PL intensities of the aqueous CQDs-NHS verse irradiation time in a cuvette are shown in (a). The cell viabilities of HEK 293 T and 16HBE at varied dose of CQDs-NHS are disclosed in (b) and (c) respectively.

Photographs of the gels of the labeled BSA with the CQDs and unlabeled BSA (the upper) as well as the corresponding fluorescent imaging (the lower). Strip (a) is a molecular-weight marker. Strip (b1∼b3) are the image of unlabeled BSA with addition volumes of 20, 5, 10 μL respectively. (c1∼c3) are the image of labeled BSA with addition volumes of 20, 5, 10 μL respectively.

simple-head.ce:title: A convenient way of activating carbon quantum dots and the efficient isolation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoluminescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attracts extensive interests. Chemical modifications in a proper way on CQDs are vital for their applications. However, isolating the modified CQDs efficiently from the mixture is still a challenge. Conventional dialysis isolation did not effect at all since the unremarkable changes in size of the modified CQDs. Herein active N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) ester was introduced chemically onto the CQDs in very mild conditions, which was demonstrated by IR and X-ray photoelectron spectra. Such activated CQDs could be extracted by ethyl acetate from the mixture efficiently and conveniently which was confirmed by photoluminescence spectra. Experimental results also demonstrated the low cytotoxicity and the high stability against photo-bleaching of the CQDs-NHS. The high reactivity of NHS moiety was demonstrated by the reaction with bovine serum albumin, which revealed in an experiment of sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electropheresis and the fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attracted many researchers to engage in the related researches and applied them in chemical analyses [1,2], organic syntheses [3], photocatalyses [4–7], cellular staining [8], etc. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials such as organic fluoresceins [9] and semiconductor quantum dots [10], CQDs have unique characters such as wonderful photo-bleaching resistance and lower toxicity. CQDs should have the promise to be used in biology on account of their low manufacturing costs and cytotoxicity as well as their photoluminescence properties and well photo-bleaching resistances. They meet the requirements of a fluorescent labeling generally. Actually, in order to realize the covalent bonding with biomolecules, CQDs should be modified chemically at first and then the modified CQDs should be isolated efficiently. In aqueous medium, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was applied universally in activating the carboxyl of grapheme quantum dots (GQDs) giving an amine-reactive O-acylisourea intermediate, which is reactive with the subsequently added N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) giving active NHS ester. Such NHS ester-modified GQDs perform high reactive with primary amino groups of biomolecules such as insulin [11]. Such a tactics was also used in synthesize peptide-GQDs conjugates [12]. However, until today, there were few reports about the efficient technique of isolating the modified CQDs from the reaction mixtures. The NHS-functionalized CQDs were involved in successive synthesis directly without any purification. That may affect the experimental results. Separating the activated CQDs from the mixture efficiently is still a challenge since the sizes of the activated CQDs are changed unremarkable. Therefore the dialysis isolation did not work at all.

Apparently, NHS can be conjugated with GQDs or CQDs in very mild conditions. The obtained NHS moieties performed reactive with primary amino of biomolecule. The CQDs derived from citric acids should possess dense functional groups of carboxyl. As long as some of these carboxyl groups are activated and the technique of isolating the NHS-activated CQDs from the reaction mixture is invented it is promised to realize the purification of such functionalized CQDs. We tried to implement such a goal and found that the CQDs derived from citric acids could be activated by NHS and the modified CQDs could be isolated by extraction operations with ethyl acetate. Hence the preparation, activation and isolation are very convenient. And the reactivity of the modified-CQDs with bovine serum albumin (BSA) was checked with experiments of sodium dodecyl sulfate polyacrylamide gel electropheresis and fluorescent imaging. Our plan was described schematically in Fig. 1 .

Citric acid monohydrate (≥99%), ethyl acetate (99%), NHS (98%), dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC, 99%) and BSA (∼66 kD, 96%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used throughout the work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CQDs and the modification on the CQDs with NHS
ce:section.ce:para: Weighed 1.002 g citric acids placed in a drying oven at 180 °C for 1 h for preparing the CQDs. Then it was removed from the oven and cooled down to room temperature. Such CQD (0.700 g) were mixed with 0.611 g of NHS and 1.402 g of DCC in 10 mL tetrahydrofuran (THF) and executed the reaction at 60 °C for 4 h. The resulted mixtures were placed in ice room of a refrigerator (−16 °C) for about 1 h. the produced precipitations were removed from the mixtures by a filtration. The filtrated solutions were then placed in the refrigerator again to wipe out the precipitations successively. Such operations were repeated to purify the product until the solution became clear. Then THF solvent was removed and 10.0 mL ethyl acetates were put in with a complete vibration. At last, 10 mL water was added with a complete shaking to dissolve the intact CQDs. Ethyl acetate extractives containing NHS-modified CQDs (CQDs-NHS) was then obtained.

Cells of HEK 293 T and 16HBE were seeded respectively in 96-well plates at a density of 5000 cells per well in triplicate for overnight incubation. After treatments with CQDs-NHS at different concentrations for 44 h cultivation, methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was added for additional 4 h incubation. The medium was discarded and dimethyl sulfoxide was added to dissolve the formazan blue, which was formed in the cells. The absorbance at 490 nm was recorded with a Thermo Scientific Multiskan spectrum microplate spectrophotometer. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation
Cell Viability (%) = Atreated/Acontrol × 100%
Where Acontrol was obtained in the absence of CQDs-NHS and Atreated was obtained at the presence of CQDs-NHS.

Fetched 5.0 mL of the above CQDs-NHS (36 mg in ethyl acetate) solutions and evaporated the solvents completely. Then 12 mL phosphate buffered solution (PBS, pH = 7.4) solutions were added in forming an emulsion of the CQDs-NHS. 4.0 mL of such emulsions were measured and mixed with 6.0 mL BSA solutions (4 mg mL−1 in PBS, pH = 7.4). The mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 8 h for the completion of luminescent labeling of BSA with the CQDs. In a gel electrophoresis experiment, 5, 10 and 20 μL of such solutions were fetched respectively and dropped in the respective wells of the gel. The results were disclosed by the photographs irradiated under an ultraviolet lamp. Then the gel was stained with coomassie brilliant blue and was taken a picture again under daylight.

The prepared CQDs were demonstrated by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN, FEI Company) and atomic force microscopes (AFM) (Veeco Instruments Inc.). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) estimation (KRATOS AMICUS) were utilized to detect the elemental composites with a mono-chromatized Al Kα X-ray source. Wide scan over a binding energy of 0–1350 eV and high-resolution scans (step = 0.05 eV) on C 1 s and N 1 s were executed to check structural alteration resulted by the chemical modification on the CQDs with NHS. Elemental intergradient was estimated by nonlinear least-square fitting using different weighting of Gaussian-Lorentzian functions. The fluorescent emissions as well as the photo-bleaching resistance of the CQDs were detected by fluorescence spectra (LS 45, PerkinElmer). The covalent bonding between the CQDs and BSA was proved by the gel electrophoresis. The gel imaging was acquired by UVP GelMax Imager System. The photographs of the gel irradiated with ultraviolet lamps and stained with coomassie brilliant were used to exclaim the fluorescent labeling reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of the CQDs with NHS and the efficient isolation
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CQDs were witnessed by Fig. 2 . Both TEM and AFM image of Fig. 2 demonstrate the resulted CQDs. Inset of Fig. 2(a) exhibits the clean crystalline planes while inset of Fig. 2(b) clearly displays the sizes of the CQDs are ranged mainly from 2.5 nm to 3.8 nm.

The as-prepared CQDs derived from citric acids should own dense surface carboxyl, which can be deduced from the strong acid of the aqueous CQDs solution. IR spectrum of Fig. 3 (a) also demonstrates the existence of the carboxyl. Band of 1710 cm−1 in Fig. 3(a) should be assigned to stretching vibration of CO (v(CO)) of the independent carboxyl groups. Some hydrogen bonds between carboxyl and hydroxyl result in the blue shifting of v(CO) located at 1774 cm−1. Band of 1627 cm−1 is attributable to v(CC) which means the intramolecular dehydration. Intermolecular dehydration is demonstrated by the asymmetrical vibration of C − OC at 1078 cm−1. The intensive and wide absorption centered at 3430 cm−1 should be due to the v(O − H) of the CQDs. The dense hydroxyls of the CQDs contribute greatly to their wonderful solubility in water. Apart from those, methylene of the CQDs can also be detected, which marked with a dashed rectangle in Fig. 3(a).

XPS with a high-resolution scan on C 1 s also provides the structural information of the CQDs, shown in Fig. 3(b). Binding energy of 289.0 eV should be due to the COOH [13]. That consists with the acidity of the CQDs. Dehydrations among the citric acids lead to the production of carbonyl and ether groups, which proved by the peak of 285.5 eV. Band 284.7 eV discloses the alkyl or ethenyl of the CQDs [14]. This exclaims the intramolecular dehydrations which contribute the production of conjugated structure of the CQDs. These rigid species along with the auxochromic groups endow the PL property of the CQDs.

NHS is commonly used in activating carboxyl [15], which is applied extensively in biological synthesis [9]. The resulted NHS ester shows high reactive to primary amino group of biomolecule under extremely mild conditions [16,17]. Herein, carboxyl of the CQDs can be activated by NHS generating the reactive NHS ester (CQDs-NHS) at the presence of DCC. The structural information of the CQDs-NHS can be disclosed by IR spectrum of Fig. 3(a). Band 1847, 1776 and 1730 cm−1 are assigned to v(CO) of the ester, asymmetrical v(CO) and symmetrical v(CO) of the NHS imide moiety respectively [16]. XPS of Fig. 3(c) exhibits the obvious alterations of C 1 s spectrum compared with that of Fig. 3(b). Binding energies of 289.5, 286.7, 285.2 and 284.7 eV are belonged to OCO, OCN, CO or CO, CC or CH respectively. Components of OCN are derived from the introduced NHS moiety. The ratio of [CC or CH]/[OCO] increases from 1.3 in Fig. 3(b) to 8.5 in Fig. 3(c). This predicts the introduction of NHS onto surfaces of the CQDs. That is also demonstrated clearly by the core-level spectrum of N 1 s, shown in Fig. 3(d).

The produced CQDs possess wonderful solubility in water or THF since there are large amount of polar functional groups on the CQDs. However, they can not be dissolved in ethyl acetates at all. This can be exclaimed easily by the photograph irradiated with a portable ultraviolet light, which displayed in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a) exhibits distinctly that the CQDs can hardly be dissolved in ethyl acetate. Fig. 4(c) reveals that the ethyl acetate extractive possesses rather weak fluorescent emission between 450 ∼ 500 nm. Such a property of the CQDs can be utilized in isolation of the intact CQDs from the CQDs-NHS solution.

CQDs can be applied as a labeling in biology, which described in several reports [18,19]. However, covalent bonding with biomolecules requires enough reactivity of the CQDs. Hence the proper chemical modifications are indispensable for the further application. The current reports about this aspect are rare according to recent publications [11,12]. The main causes, I think, should be attributed to the difficulty of separating the products from reaction mixtures. There are some reports about the separation methods with dialysis bag. However, this method is not effective according to our practices since the as-prepared CQDs can penetrate the dialysis bag even the dialysis bag of 1000 MW is used. Therefore, developing an effective method of isolating the modified CQDs from the mixture is quite necessary for the applications.

Originally, the CQDs can hardly be resolved in ethyl acetate. However, the solubility of the CQDs changes distinctively on account of the introduction of the NHS esters on the CQDs. The CQDs-NHS dissolve well in ethyl acetate, which exhibited in Fig. 4(b). This should be attributed to the introduced NHS ester groups. Such phenomenon can be utilized in isolating the produced CQDs-NHS from the extra CQDs. The PL properties of the two layers are easily judged by irradiation of a portable ultraviolet lamp. This suggests that the intact CQDs can be removed by washings of water. Apparently, extraction with ethyl acetate combining with water washing is an efficient way of purifying the obtained CQDs-NHS. This approach ensures the intact CQDs are isolated completely from the CQDs-NHS. Even the surface carboxyl groups of the CQDs can be converted into NHS esters in this case there are some hydrophilic hydroxyl groups on the surface which lead to the partly dissolution of the CQDs-NHS in aqueous medium. Hence it may bring about part losses of the CQDs-NHS according to this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo-bleaching resistance of the CQDs and cytotoxicity of the CQDs-NHS
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know that organic fluoresceins are applied extensively in labeling of biomolecules such as fluorescent dyes of Alexa Fluor series, Cy3, Cy5, etc. However, such organic dyes are apt to be photo-bleached under a long time irradiation of ultraviolet lights that reduces the stability of the fluorescent imaging. Thus the photo-bleaching resistance is vital in some cases. The CQDs possess a well steadiness against photo-bleaching, which revealed in Fig. 5 (a). Within the lasting irradiation of 60 min under ultraviolet lights, the PL intensity declines about 6%. That performs better than Cy5 at this point [9].

The cytotoxicity analysis is very important for the application of the CQDs-NHS especially in vivo. This is examined with MTT assay. Cells of HEK 293 T and 16HBE are used in the experiment and the cell viabilities are revealed in Fig. 5(b) and (c). The CQDs-NHS show little toxicity even at a higher concentration ranges [20]. Fig. 5(b) and (c) exhibits 89.7% and 98.8% cell viability at 500 μg mL−1 for HEK 293 T and 16HBE respectively. This demonstrates the safety and biocompatibility of the CQDs-NHS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent labeling on BSA with the CQDs-NHS
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs-NHS can be resolved by PBS (pH = 7.4) and react with primary amino groups of BSA to realize the fluorescent labeling. The result is presented in Fig. 6 . It is obvious that the CQDs can be attached with BSA covalently. The marker strip (Fig. 6(a)) shows that the molecular weight of the experimental protein (∼ 66 kD). The unlabeled BSA do not emit fluorescence (the fluorescent imaging of Fig. 6(b1–b3)) while the labeled BSA emit fluorescence under irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp (the fluorescent imaging of Fig. 6(c1–c3)). Additionally, Fig. 6 tells us that all the BSA are labeled by the fluorescent CQDs and their covalent combinations. Due to the attached CQDs, the molecular weight of the BSA increases leading to a slower electrophoresis velocity shown in Fig. 6(c1–c3). Those results prove that the activated CQDs with NHS have great potential in fluorescent labeling of biomolecules with primary amino groups.

The NHS-activated CQDs are isolated efficiently from the reaction mixture by extraction of ethyl acetate. This provides a convenient strategy of utilizing the photo-luminescence CQDs in fluorescent labeling of biological molecules. The non-toxicity, photo-bleaching resistance, easy manufacture and lower manufacturing cost have great superiorities than those of organic fluoresceins or some semiconductor quantum dots (CdSe, CdS, etc.) [21].

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1508085MB31). The authors gratefully acknowledge very helpful discussions with Dr. Zi Liu and her assistances during the biological experiments.
